origin: A short scriptural genealogy passage in Ancient/Koine Greek, recounting the lineage from Jeconiah to Shealtiel and then to Zerubbabel “after the captivity of Babylon.” This is characteristic of the New Testament’s Matthean genealogy (Matthew 1:12) and/or a Greek biblical manuscript tradition reflecting the Septuagint/Koine style.
author: Most likely the author/attributed source is the Gospel writer traditionally credited as Matthew (or the compiler/translator working in the Matthean genealogical framework). If not directly Matthew himself, it could be a later Greek biblical translator/scribe reproducing the Matthean text.
thoughts: The author’s perspective appears matter-of-fact and providential: the motivation is to establish a divinely meaningful lineage and historical continuity after the Babylonian exile. The terse “begat” structure suggests an intent to document fulfillment of prophecy and legitimacy of ancestry rather than to explain events or characters in depth.
audience: Greek-speaking Jewish/early Christian readers who recognize the exile-and-lineage framework—people interested in Jesus’ ancestry, messianic fulfillment, and scriptural authority (readers of the Greek Bible or communities using Greek texts).
written_when: Likely in the 1st century CE (commonly dated for the Gospel of Matthew), with the specific wording reflecting Koine Greek usage typical of that period or later manuscript transmission within early Christian textual history.
